login.title: Login
login.loginFailed: Login failed. Please check your credentials.
login.usernameMinLength: Username must be at least 3 characters long
login.passwordMinLength: Password must be at least 6 characters long

analyze.selectPlace: Click on a place in the map to view additional information

allCategoriesDialog.title: Activities
allCategoriesDialog.clickHint: Click on any activity to view more detailed information
allCategoriesDialog.relevanceInfo: Activity relevance indicates how frequently an activity is visited. The value is derived from the “Mobility in Germany 2023” study and varies by persona group and regional class. Note: At county and state level, the relevance may deviate slightly from the actual weighting.

personasDialog.info:
The persona groups originate from the “Mobility in Germany 2023” study and can feel a bit confusing at first. Each persona represents a typical mix of activities and how important they are for that group.

The note about car availability only indicates whether a car could in principle be used. If no car is chosen in the actual mode choice, the activity mix still remains unchanged and therefore differs from a person who never has a car available. What matters is not the car use on a specific day, but the general availability of a car.

The importance of the individual persona groups varies by region.

analyze.startComparisonHint: Select another area on the map with a right-click to start comparing!

placesDialog.title: Map showing locations of activities
placesDialog.disabledForCountiesStates: The places view is disabled for counties and states. Please select the municipality/hexagon view for a more detailed analysis about an area
placesDialog.categoryRelevanceInfo: The percentage shows how relevant the single Activity is for the whole Category.

analysis.title: Analysis Top 5 Activities
analysis.qualityRating: Quality rating: The quality rating shows the quality of mobility in different categories from A (best quality) to F (lowest quality).

introduction.title: How to understanding activities
introduction.description: Every trip ends with an activity. This could be the desire to visit a restaurant, to spend time at a museum, or to go shopping. Activities are thus the reason to start trips and where mobility comes into play.
introduction.focus: mobi.mapr focuses on everyday mobility. Commutes to educational institutions and workplaces are intentionally not considered. Instead, the focus is on day-to-day destinations such as errands, leisure activities, or social visits.

dataSource.title: Data Source: Mobility in Germany (MiD)
dataSource.description: The activities used in mobi.mapr are based on the 'Mobilität in Deutschland' (Mobility in Germany) study. Data from the 2023 survey is currently used.
dataSource.survey: In this nationwide survey, people are asked, among other things:
dataSource.survey1: Which routes they take in everyday life
dataSource.survey3: What are the reasons for these journeys
dataSource.result: The result is a list of approximately 30 activity categories. These categories are deliberately formulated in general terms in order to be evaluated statistically.

translation.title: Translation into concrete everyday activities
translation.description: The activity categories collected by the MiD are relatively abstract. Therefore, they are translated into concrete everyday destinations in mobi.mapr based on scientific literature and empirical assumptions.
translation.details: For each activity, the following is stored:
translation.details1: Which concrete destination locations are assigned to it
translation.details2: How these destination locations are weighted
translation.viewInfo: This information can be viewed by clicking on the respective activity.

ambiguities.title: Dealing with Ambiguities
ambiguities.description: Not every activity can be translated unambiguously and precisely.
ambiguities.example: An example is the activity 'Walking the dog' from the MiD study. This actually describes movement in public space without a clear destination. In mobi.mapr, it is currently approximated through destinations such as:
ambiguities.validation: Validations show, however, that such ambiguities often balance out in the bigger picture. This means: Even if individual activities can only be approximated, the overall picture of everyday mobility remains stable and reliable.

weighting.title: Weighting of Activities
weighting.description: The importance of individual activities depends strongly on the respective group of people.
weighting.example2: Adults have a stronger focus on shopping or errands.
weighting.example3: Seniors have different activity patterns compared to other groups.
weighting.standardView: In the standard view of mobi.mapr, all groups of people are initially considered together. This creates an average activity profile.

regional.title: Regional Differentiation
regional.description: In addition to differentiation per groups of people, activities are also weighted regionally.
regional.meaning1: In metropolitan regions, destination structures and preferences differ significantly from those in rural areas.
regional.meaning2: City dwellers seek urban offerings such as gastronomy, cultural activities, or specialized services more frequently.
regional.meaning3: In rural areas, other facilities and provisions are prioritized.
regional.consideration: These regional differences are taken into account in mobi.mapr to make realistic statements about everyday mobility.

legend.title: Legend
legend.introduction: Mobility can be viewed in two different ways: as mobility time or as mobility quality. Both perspectives complement one another – one is concrete and tangible, the other comparative and contextual.

quality.title: Mobility Quality
quality.description: Mobility quality sets the calculated mobility time in relation to a structurally comparable region.
quality.comparison: Travel time in a city is thus compared with other cities – not with rural areas. Similarly, a rural region is compared with other rural regions.
quality.fair: This creates a fair and context-based assessment.
quality.perception: Another important aspect: Time differences in the lower range are perceived much more sensitively than in the upper range. The difference between 5 and 10 minutes feels more significant than the difference between 45 and 50 minutes.
quality.scaleIntro: Therefore, the mobility quality scale is structured exponentially:
quality.scaleLower: In the lower range, finer differentiation occurs.
quality.scaleUpper: In the upper range, a coarser one.
quality.result: The result is an index value that is additionally translated into an understandable grade. This transforms an abstract number into an intuitive assessment of the mobility situation.
quality.howToRead: The colors on the map show the quality of mobility from A (best quality) to F (lowest quality).

time.title: Mobility Time
time.description: Mobility time describes the actual evaluated travel time in minutes – including all penalties (see info box transport modes and rating) as well as the weighting of multiple reachable destinations.
time.result: The result is a time specification in minutes. However, this is not a purely technical measurement, but rather a perceived time.
time.perceived: This may differ from the actual driven or walked time – but it reflects the actual decision-making reality much better.
time.example: Example: For bicycles, depending on the municipality, a certain 'initial resistance' is taken into account. In practice, you don't get on the bike for every short distance. 'For me to take the bike, the destination must be at least 15 minutes away – otherwise I'd rather walk here given the traffic.'
time.conclusion: Mobility time thus reflects which means of transport is actually chosen under realistic conditions.
time.scaleDescription: The scale is structured linearly and is given in minutes.
time.howToRead: The colors on the map show mobility time from dark blue (short times, 0-15 minutes) to light blue (long times, over 60 minutes). The darker the color, the shorter the mobility time.

transportModes.title: Transport Modes
transportModes.description: Select the transport modes that should be considered in the calculation. You can activate multiple transport modes at once; at least one mode must always remain selected.
transportModes.availableOptionsDescription: Each transport mode can be individually turned on or off to adapt the analysis to your requirements.
transportModes.cannotDeselectLast: At least one transport mode must remain selected.
transportModes.introduction: Choose from four transport modes to analyze mobility in your region. Each mode uses sophisticated routing algorithms to provide realistic travel time calculations.

pedestrian.title: Pedestrian
pedestrian.description: Walking routes are calculated using realistic pedestrian paths, taking into account sidewalks, pedestrian zones, and walking-friendly infrastructure.

bicycle.title: Bicycle
bicycle.description: Bicycle routing is optimized for average city bicycles, prioritizing dedicated bicycle paths and lanes for safer and more efficient cycling routes.

car.title: Car
car.description: Car routing considers road networks, traffic patterns, and realistic driving conditions to calculate accurate travel times.

transit.title: Transit
transit.description: Public transportation routing uses real transit schedules and connections to find the best routes using buses, trains, trams, and other public transport options.

valhalla.title: Valhalla Routing (Walk, Bike, Car)
valhalla.description: Pedestrian, bicycle, and car routes are calculated using Valhalla, a powerful open-source routing engine that provides realistic and accurate pathfinding.
valhalla.feature1: Hills, elevation changes, and terrain are factored into route calculations
valhalla.feature2: Bicycle routes prioritize dedicated bicycle paths and lanes when available
valhalla.feature3: Realistic routing considers actual road conditions and infrastructure

motis.title: Motis Transit Routing
motis.description: Public transportation routes are calculated using Motis, which processes real transit schedules and timetables to find optimal connections.
motis.feature1: Calculations are based on a reference Tuesday schedule for consistent results
motis.feature2: Each activity has a default time assigned (e.g., restaurants at 19:00), and transit is calculated to arrive at the optimal time
motis.feature3: The best connection is selected automatically, with no waiting time at the destination counted

general.title: General
general.intro: To reach an activity, you need a mode of transport – this can be your own feet, a bicycle, a car, or public transit.
general.pedestrianBase: At least one transport mode must be selected for the analysis. You can choose any combination of modes that matches your scenario (for example only cycling and public transport, without walking as a separate profile).
general.questionIntro: By selecting transport modes, you essentially answer the following question:
general.question: How good is my mobility quality when I'm traveling on foot versus when I have a bicycle available?
general.approach: mobi.mapr takes a realistic approach: it always selects the mode of transport that is fastest overall, taking all factors into account—including parking, stopping, and other associated factors. Even if a car is available, people usually prefer to walk to a bakery 200 meters away, regardless if the actual driving time would be shorter. The additional effort required to find parking and get out and back to the location makes the car unattractive in this case.

penalties.title: Penalties
penalties.intro: In order to compare modes of transport fairly, penalties (time surcharges) are applied to the pure travel time.
penalties.example1: For cars, depending on the time of the trip to an activity, a small time penalty is considered to realistically represent parking search.
penalties.example2: For bicycles, one minute is always added for the locking/unlocking or carrying the bicycle to the road to begin the trip.
penalties.example3: For cargo bikes, three minutes are added, as searching for parking for the larger vehicle becomes more complex.
penalties.subjective: In addition, routes can feel subjectively longer or shorter. In a city with well-developed bicycle infrastructure, a route often feels shorter than while traveling on a heavily trafficked main road.
penalties.adfc: To include such factors, we have integrated, among other things, the ADFC bicycle climate test.
penalties.documentation: You can find a detailed explanation in our documentation.

profiles.title: Mobility Profiles
profiles.variation: A transport mode can vary greatly. A cargo bike, a trekking bike, or a road bike all belong to the 'bicycle' category, but differ significantly in their behavior and usage.
profiles.mapping: In mobi.mapr, these differences are mapped through so-called mobility profiles. The most frequently used profile is used by default in the available view.
profiles.future: In the future, it will be possible to create and use individual profiles. Stay tuned!

routing.title: Routing
routing.complexity: Finding a route between start and destination is significantly more complex than simply following a street. Inclines, traffic lights, or intersections can significantly prolong the actual travel time.
routing.fullRouting: In order to take as many influencing factors as possible into account, each route is routed in its entirety.
routing.startPoint: The starting point is the center of the respective hexagon.
routing.destination: The destination is the specific activity.
routing.engines: We use the FOSS routing engine Valhalla for routing pedestrian, bicycle, and car traffic. For public transport, we use the MOTIS project.
routing.transitDay: Public transport routing is performed on a representative Tuesday. Each activity has a defined time at which the route is calculated.
routing.waitingTime: Waiting time at the destination is not taken into account. Example: If a meeting is planned for 19:00, but the train arrives at 18:40, the journey is considered complete upon arrival – the remaining waiting time is not included in the evaluation.

mobilityRating.title: Rating
mobilityRating.description: Choose between two valuation methods for mobility analysis.
mobilityRating.introduction: To evaluate mobility in a neutral and comparable way, a clear and comprehensible methodology is needed.

time.label: Mobility Time
time.title: Time-Based Rating
time.description: Time-based rating calculates absolute travel times to destinations, taking into account route penalties and mode-specific factors.
time.step1: From a starting point, the next three instances of each activity (e.g., restaurants) are calculated for all enabled transport modes.
time.step2: Each route receives penalties depending on the means of transport, such as parking search time for cars or bicycle infrastructure quality (ADFC-Fahrradklimatest) for bicycles.
time.step3: For the chosen mode mix, the best three routes are selected and weighted together. The weighting depends on the activity type - one prefers more choices for a restaurant than for a post office.
time.example: Example: The best route might be by foot, the second best by bicycle, and the third best by car. All three are combined with activity-specific weightings.

quality.label: Quality
quality.title: Quality-Based Rating
quality.description: Quality-based rating evaluates mobility performance relative to similar municipalities, providing a comparative perspective rather than absolute time values.
quality.example: Example: If one municipality needs 15 minutes to reach a supermarket, but similar municipalities (by RegioStar) need 20 minutes on average, they only need 75% of the time. This relative performance is then translated into a quality grade.
quality.graphDescription: The relative time percentage (e.g., 75%) is mapped to a quality grade (A+ to F-) using an exponential function. Lower percentages based on the average (better performance) result in higher grades.

mobilityTime.title: Mobility Time
mobilityTime.approach: The fundamental approach is always the same.
mobilityTime.exampleIntro: For example, to calculate the mobility quality for the activity 'Supermarket' using a transport mode combination (e.g., foot + bicycle), the following steps are performed:

step1.title: Routing to all relevant destinations
step1.description: From a defined starting point, the route to all supermarkets in the area is calculated for each available transport mode.

step2.title: Adding penalties
step2.description: Additional time penalties are added to the pure travel time, such as for parking search or infrastructure quality.

step3.title: Selection and weighting of the best options
step3.description: From all calculated routes, the three best connections are selected and weighted together. The weighting means how 'important' the respective destination is for the mobility quality or how important it is to have different choices.
step3.explanation: The background is that not only does the nearest destination counts – a certain amount of choice increases the actual mobility quality. The specific weighting depends on the respective activity. For restaurants, choice is more important than, for example, a parcel station.

mobilityTime.conclusion: For this reason, we deliberately do not speak of travel time, but of mobility time.

mobilityQuality.title: Mobility Quality
mobilityQuality.weakness: Mobility time is intuitively understandable, but has a weakness: It only allows limited comparisons between different regions or activity types.
mobilityQuality.example: Is a 20-minute travel time to a hairdresser in a rural area good or bad?
mobilityQuality.introduction: To answer such questions, mobility quality is introduced. Here, the calculated mobility time is set in relation to a reference value.
mobilityQuality.calculation: Thanks to comparison data available throughout Germany, it can be determined, for example, that in rural areas an average of 30 minutes is needed to reach a hairdresser. If your own mobility time is 20 minutes, this results in a mobility quality of 66%.
mobilityQuality.gradeIntro: To make this abstract percentage value more understandable, it is additionally translated into a grade.
mobilityQuality.scale1: In the lower range, fine-grained distinctions are made.
mobilityQuality.scale2: In the upper range, a coarser differentiation takes place.
mobilityQuality.conclusion: This creates a comparable, intuitively comprehensible rating standard.

activeFilters.title: Current Filters
activeFilters.description: This overview shows the currently active filters and their count. The numbers show how many filters are selected in each category.
activeFilters.introduction: Filters allow you to customize the analysis by selecting specific activities, personas, regions, and regional types. Use the filter button to adjust these settings.
activeFilters.adjustFiltersDescription: Click on 'Filter' to open and adjust the filter settings. There you can select activities, personas, RegioStars, and states.
activeFilters.midProjectDescription: Since this is a MID project, you can additionally filter activities and personas to further refine the analysis.
activeFilters.activitiesDescription: Activities are based on the study 'Mobilität in Deutschland' (Mobility in Germany). They group different real-life activities (e.g., supermarket) into abstract daily needs categories, such as 'daily needs'.
activeFilters.personasDescription: A persona defines how relevant certain activities are for different user groups. For example, going to a playground is normally more relevant for a child than for an adult without children. Personas also come from the MID study.
activeFilters.regioStarsDescription: If you only want to view certain regions in Germany, for example rural areas, you can filter this here. It does not change the data itself, such as states, but it does filter the results. This is then also reflected in the Top rankings.
activeFilters.statesDescription: States allow you to filter for regions on the map. While this does not influence the data at all, it allows for better comparison, as well as filtering the Top rankings.
activeFilters.filterButtonDisabledHint: The adjustment of filters is only available for registered users.

generalContent.title: Filter Overview
generalContent.intro: In addition to selecting transport modes, choosing destinations, i.e., activities, is crucial. These can be adjusted using the 'Filter' button.
generalContent.mid23: The activities are based on the 'Mobilität in Deutschland 2023' (MiD23) study and are used with their kind permission.
generalContent.personas: Additionally, activities can be restricted for individual persona groups. To learn more about each, click on the respective tabs.
generalContent.combinations: The arbitrary combination of 30 activities, 10 personas, and 4 transport mode combinations results in an exponentially large number of possibilities to create the map; at the end, there are more than one trillion possibilities.
generalContent.beta: To avoid overloading computing capacity and prevent misuse, these functions are only available for registered users. This feature is currently in the beta phase.
generalContent.regionalFilters: Additionally, states and regional classes can be selected. These are not content-based but superficial filters.
generalContent.example: For example, if you want to know what the best municipalities in rural areas in Baden-Württemberg are, you can filter here and it will be displayed in the panel on the right.

introduction.title: Understanding Activities
introduction.description: Every trip ends with an activity. This could be visiting a restaurant, spending time at a museum, or going shopping. Activities represent the actual purpose of mobility.
introduction.focus: mobi.mapr focuses on day-to-day mobility. Commutes to educational institutions and workplaces are intentionally not considered. Instead, the focus is on everyday destinations such as shopping, leisure activities, or social visits.

dataSource.title: Data Source: Mobility in Germany (MiD)
dataSource.description: The activities used in mobi.mapr are based on the 'Mobilität in Deutschland' (Mobility in Germany) study. Data from 2023 are used.
dataSource.survey: In this nationwide survey, people are asked, among other things:
dataSource.survey1: Which routes they take in everyday life
dataSource.survey3: What are the reasons for these journeys
dataSource.result: The result is a list of approximately 30 activity categories. These categories are deliberately formulated in general terms to be statistically evaluable.

translation.title: Translation into Concrete Everyday Activities
translation.description: The activity categories collected in MiD are relatively abstract. Therefore, they are translated into concrete everyday destinations in mobi.mapr based on scientific literature and empirical assumptions.
translation.details: For each activity, the following is stored:
translation.details1: Which concrete destination locations are assigned to it
translation.details2: How these destination locations are weighted
translation.viewInfo: This information can be viewed by clicking on the respective activity.

ambiguities.title: Dealing with Ambiguities
ambiguities.description: Not every activity can be translated unambiguously and precisely.
ambiguities.example: An example is the activity 'Walking the dog' from the MiD study. This actually describes movement in public space without a clear destination. In mobi.mapr, it is currently approximated through destinations such as:
ambiguities.validation: Validations show, however, that such ambiguities often balance out in the bigger picture. This means: Even if individual activities can only be approximated, the overall picture of everyday mobility remains stable and reliable.

regional.title: Regional Differentiation
regional.description: In addition to differentiation by groups of people, activities are also weighted regionally.
regional.meaning1: In metropolitan regions, destination structures and preferences differ significantly from those in rural areas.
regional.meaning2: City dwellers seek urban offerings such as gastronomy, culture, or specialized services more frequently.
regional.meaning3: In rural areas, other facilities and supply structures are prioritized.
regional.consideration: These regional differences are taken into account in mobi.mapr to make realistic statements about everyday mobility.

introduction.title: About Personas
introduction.description: Personas map different everyday perspectives on mobility. The personas used are based on the 10 person groups from the 'Mobilität in Deutschland (MiD)' study and enable mobility times and ratings to be displayed separately by user groups. By selecting and deselecting individual personas, differences between life realities can be made immediately visible.

personGroups.title: Groups of people according to MiD:
personGroups.group1: Working person with an available car
personGroups.group2: Working person without an available car
personGroups.group3: Non-working person with an available car
personGroups.group4: Non-working person without an available car
personGroups.group9: Pupil aged 11 and older with an available car
personGroups.group10: Pupil aged 11 and older without an available car

weighting.title: Weighting of Activities
weighting.description: The importance of individual activities depends strongly on the respective group of people.
weighting.example2: Adults have a stronger focus on shopping or errands.
weighting.example3: Seniors have different activity patterns compared to other groups.
weighting.standardView: In the standard view of mobi.mapr, all groups of people are initially considered together. This creates an average activity profile.

introduction.title: RegioStaR7: The Key to Fair Comparison
introduction.description: To realistically and differentiatedly represent mobility quality, mobi.mapr uses the RegioStaR7 classification (Regional Statistical Spatial Typology) from the Federal Ministry of Transport and Digital Infrastructure (BMDV). This typology divides Germany into seven spatial types.

types.title: The seven spatial types:
types.type2: Urban region – Regiopolis and large city
types.type3: Urban region – Medium-sized city, urbanised area
types.type4: Urban region – Small town area, village area
types.type6: Rural region – Medium-sized city, urbanised area
types.type7: Rural region – Small town, village area

usage.title: How mobi.mapr uses RegioStaR
usage.description: The RegioStaR classification flows into two central areas of analysis:
usage.point1: Average travel times: The time required to perform an everyday activity in different spatial types
usage.point2: Activities of a persona: The importance of individual activities depending on spatial type
usage.example: By integrating RegioStaR classes, it is ensured that regional differences are balanced and comparisons always remain regionally fair – so a metropolis is not compared with a rural municipality, but only regions of similar structure with each other.

dataBasis.title: The Data Basis
dataBasis.description: For the calculations, mobi.mapr uses two central data sources:
dataBasis.source1: MiD data (Mobility in Germany) for typical activities such as shopping or leisure
dataBasis.source2: OpenStreetMap data to represent, for example, the accessibility of supermarkets or other locations
dataBasis.fallback: If data for certain regions is missing, mobi.mapr uses the average of the the other regional classes.

notes.title: Notes
notes.description: The RegioStaR classification was originally a precise tool for dividing areas into different categories. However, we now know that its current application is not entirely appropriate or up to date in all cases. As with all components of mobi.mapr, we are continuously developing and integrating current data and new classifications.

statesContent.title: Federal States
statesContent.description: The filter for federal states covers the 16 German federal states and allows the analysis to be filtered by individual state.
statesContent.influence: This filtering influences the rankings and allows mobility qualities to be compared specifically by federal state.

mail.title: About mobi.mapr
mail.developedBy: This project is developed and continuously improved by the Baden-Württemberg Institute for Sustainable Mobility (BWIM).

background.title: Background
background.intro: Mobility is a complex topic that is hardly measurable. Every person is, travels, and acts individually. Nevertheless, overall trends can be identified. People want to reach different activities, whether it's a restaurant, the supermarket, or meeting friends, because mobility is a basic need.
background.approach: mobi.mapr addresses this topic by placing activities at the center. Which activities can be reached how? At the core are the open data from OpenStreetMap. If it's about more than one activity, studies are considered to aggregate them.
background.humanCentric: mobi.mapr is more than an accessibility analysis. People are at the center. Subjective factors complement pure travel times, additional penalties, such as parking search, bring the times closer to reality.
background.explore: Feel free to explore the website, discover mobility, and find out where opportunities still exist.

license.title: License and Usage
license.frontend: mobi.mapr follows a dual-track approach: The frontend is open source under the AGPL-3.0 license. We want to promote the open-source community, which means that every change must also be open source. Commercial use is not possible.
license.data: However, we understand that mobility is also a business field. We therefore pursue a different open data strategy. All data published by the mobi.mapr team is licensed under CC-BY-4.0, so it can be used commercially without disclosure. Only the BWIM must be attributed and we appreciate a link to the application.
license.note: Please note: For the geo data in the application (e.g., for district boundaries), additional licenses may apply. Further detailed information can be found in the bottom left of the dashboard, as well as in the API license file.

contact.title: Contact
contact.website: You can find more information about the project, as well as exciting analyses, on our website:
contact.email: If you have questions or suggestions, we'd love to hear from you:
contact.github: If something in the frontend doesn't work or you have suggestions, feel free to create a new issue:

mail.sourceCode: You can find the frontend source code here:
mail.apiDocumented: Our API is also openly documented, so you can use the data and functions for your own projects.

questionMark.title: Help
questionMark.infoButtons: In the various panels, you'll find info buttons that provide detailed information about the respective functions and settings. Just click on the info icon!
questionMark.moreOnWebsite: For more comprehensive information and background, feel free to visit our website.
questionMark.faq: Frequently asked questions can be found in our FAQ:

feedback.title: Send Feedback
feedback.problemPlaceholder: Please describe the problem or share your feedback...
feedback.success: Thank you for your feedback! It has been sent successfully.
feedback.error: Error sending feedback. Please try again later.
feedback.emailInvalid: Please enter a valid email address

acknowledgment.title: Acknowledgments
acknowledgment.intro: This application is made possible through the use of numerous exceptional open-source projects and data sources. Without these foundational technologies and the dedicated communities behind them, this tool would not have been feasible.

osm.title: OpenStreetMap
osm.description: We extend our sincere gratitude to all contributors of OpenStreetMap, who continuously maintain and update the geographic database that forms the foundation of this project. The comprehensive and freely accessible map data provided by the OpenStreetMap community is essential to our work.

motis.title: MOTIS
motis.description: Public transit routing capabilities are powered by the MOTIS project, an open-source multi-modal journey planner. Without MOTIS, this application would not be able to provide accurate public transportation routing and analysis.

components.title: Additional Components
components.description: In addition to the projects mentioned above, this application utilizes numerous other open-source libraries, frameworks, and tools. A comprehensive list of these components is provided below.

frontend.title: Frontend Application
frontend.description: The frontend application of mobi.mapr is licensed under the GNU Affero General Public License version 3.0 (AGPL-3.0). The complete source code is publicly available on GitHub and may be freely used, modified, and distributed in accordance with the terms of the AGPL-3.0 license.

data.title: Data Published by mobi.mapr
data.description: All data generated and published by the mobi.mapr team in publicly available projects are licensed under the Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0). This license applies exclusively to data published by the mobi.mapr team in public projects.

thirdParty.title: Third-Party Data
thirdParty.description: Please note that additional data sources may be subject to different licensing terms. In particular, geographic data such as city district boundaries (shapefiles) obtained from municipal and regional authorities must be credited appropriately when used. The specific licensing terms for each data source are indicated in the component listings below.

email.label: Email
email.hint: We will send the download link to this address when the export is ready.
email.invalid: Please enter a valid email address.

includePopulation.label: Include population data
includePopulation.description: Adds population fields to the export where supported by the API.

success.title: Export started
success.lead: Your export is running on the server right now.
success.durationHint: How long it takes can vary a lot depending on the selected region, activities, profiles, and resolution.
success.cacheHint: If the same resolution was requested recently, you may sometimes receive the download link very quickly.
success.message: When the file is ready, you will receive a download link at {{email}}.
success.footer: You can close this dialog. Please check your spam folder if you do not see the message.

license.title: License Notice
license.ccBy: What does this mean? CC BY 4.0 (Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International):

mobile.hint: For a detailed analysis, please open this site on a desktop.

info.title: Statistics
info.description: This overview shows the best rankings based on the selected level (States, Counties, or Municipalities) and the current filters and evaluation criteria.
info.aboutData: The data displayed represents the top rankings based on the selected level and the current filters and evaluation criteria.
info.layerSelectionDescription: Select the geographic level for which rankings should be displayed: States, Counties, or Municipalities.
info.prefiltersDescription: The best rankings automatically reflect all active filters on the left side, including selected States and RegioStars. Rankings are adjusted according to these filters.
info.tiedRanksDescription: When multiple areas have the same value, they share the same rank:
info.tiedRanksRule1: Areas with identical values receive the same rank position
info.tiedRanksRule2: The next rank position is skipped accordingly (e.g., if two areas share 3rd place, the next will be 5th place)
info.countyDefinition: With 'Kreis' (county) we mean both urban and rural districts. Since this is not uniformly regulated nationwide, we do not distinguish between them
info.largeCountySplit: For better comparability, counties that consist of only one municipality with more than 300,000 inhabitants are divided into their districts. The county (e.g., Berlin) is then under 'Kreis' and B-Spandau accordingly under 'Gemeinde'
info.qualityViewHint: In the quality view, area units are grouped and evaluated by their index (see info on mobility evaluation). This means area units with the same grade (e.g., C+) can be on different positions in the ranking.

PREPARING_PROJECT.TITLE: Preparing Project
PREPARING_PROJECT.MESSAGE: Please wait while the project is being loaded...
PREPARING_PROJECT.COFFEE_MESSAGE: Grab a coffee! Depending on the complexity of the filters, this can take a couple of minutes.

FUN_FACTS.TITLE: Did You Know?
FUN_FACTS.FACT_1: Germany has over 9,000 train stations that are used daily by millions of people.
FUN_FACTS.FACT_2: The German rail network extends over more than 38,000 kilometers.
FUN_FACTS.FACT_3: Public transportation significantly contributes to reducing CO2 emissions.
FUN_FACTS.FACT_4: Mobility analysis helps plan the best transportation infrastructure.
FUN_FACTS.FACT_5: Walking is the most environmentally friendly way to get around.

ACHIEVEMENTS.ONE_TIME_TEXT: Preparing a project only needs to be done once.
ACHIEVEMENTS.FILTER_TEXT: This dialogue only opens after changing some filter parameters.
ACHIEVEMENTS.UNIQUE_TEXT: If you see this screen, nobody has ever produced a map like this (at least in the last 30 days).
ACHIEVEMENTS.STATS_TEXT: You can combine the 28 activities freely, for 8 different mode combinations and 7 personas, this means more than 15 billion different maps to produce.
